evals.prompt: Review the public library code in evals/files/inventory.rs as a senior Rust reviewer. Deliver outputs/review.md with severity-ranked findings (correctness first, style last), each with line evidence, why the current shape is risky, and the idiomatic direction. Then deliver outputs/inventory_fixed.rs applying the fixes.
evals.expected_output: Findings covering unwrap panics in library code, String error type, primitive obsession, needless clones, blanket lint suppression, and blocking sleep inside async; fixed code uses domain types, borrowed inputs, a domain error enum, and non-blocking sleep.
expectations: Flags the unwrap() calls on fallible operations in library code (missing SKU lookup, fs read, parse, and the network/parse unwraps in sync_remote) as panics that must become errors propagated to the caller
Flags Result<_, String> as a stringly error type; fix defines a domain error enum (thiserror-style or manual) since this is a library, not an application
Flags primitive obsession: sku/warehouse/path/url passed as owned String; fix uses newtypes (e.g. Sku) and borrowed inputs (&str, impl AsRef<Path>)
Flags the needless .clone() calls on sku/path where a borrow suffices
Flags the blanket #![allow(dead_code, unused_variables, clippy::all)] as broad suppression; exceptions need #[expect(..., reason)] or removal
Flags std::thread::sleep inside the async fn as blocking the executor; fix uses the runtime's async sleep
Flags the unused warehouse parameter and the unchecked parts[1] indexing on malformed CSV lines
review.md presents findings in severity order: correctness items (panics, blocking in async, unchecked indexing) before API-shape items (error type, ownership), with style nits last or omitted

evals.prompt: Design the public API described in evals/files/retry_api_brief.md. Deliver outputs/lib.rs (public types, constructors, doc comments; internals may be todo!()) and outputs/api_notes.md explaining the shape and the semver/extension story, including how tracing support and new backoff strategies land without a breaking change.
evals.expected_output: Builder-based config, generic over the caller's error type, a structured RetryError enum distinguishing exhausted/non-retryable/timeout, non_exhaustive extension points, and doc examples.
expectations: Uses a builder (or equivalent fluent constructor) for the many-optional-knobs config rather than a positional constructor or a fully public struct
Error type is a structured enum distinguishing Exhausted (carrying the last error), NonRetryable (carrying it), and Timeout — not a String or a single opaque variant
API is generic over the caller's error type; the retryable predicate composes with it (no forced Box<dyn Error> in the caller's face)
Extension story is concrete: #[non_exhaustive] on the error enum and/or sealed or non-exhaustive backoff strategy type, and tracing planned as an additive cargo feature
Public items carry doc comments and at least one runnable-shaped example matching the known-consumer snippet

api_notes.md justifies the shape with concrete misuse-resistance arguments: what invalid configurations are unrepresentable or rejected by build(), and why inputs are borrowed vs owned — not a restatement of the type list

evals.prompt: Audit evals/files/ring_buffer.rs (Rust 2021 edition) for soundness. The type is handed to a C metrics library whose callback may fire on any thread. Deliver outputs/audit.md naming each soundness hole with line evidence and the violated invariant, and outputs/ring_buffer_fixed.rs with a sound version (safe API preferred; any remaining unsafe must state its invariant). Say how you would verify the fix.
evals.expected_output: Audit finding the out-of-bounds/wrapping read in last_two, the unjustified manual Send/Sync impls combined with cross-thread aliasing via the C callback, the escaping reference lifetime, and the pointless unsafe in push; fixed version uses safe indexing, synchronization for cross-thread access, and mentions Miri.
expectations: Finds that last_two computes head.wrapping_sub(2), which underflows when head < 2, producing an out-of-bounds from_raw_parts read; also notes the slice is wrong when the two samples wrap around the ring boundary (non-contiguous) or len < 2

Finds that register_with_c leaks a *const RingBuffer to a callback that outlives the borrow and may fire on any thread, while Rust code can still call push(&mut self) — aliasing a mutable object across threads with no synchronization

Flags the manual unsafe impl Send/Sync as unjustified assertions (precise answers may note they are inert as written since the type is auto-Send/Sync — the race actually arrives via the pointer escape); fix removes them and makes any cross-thread sharing genuinely synchronized (Mutex/atomics or an Arc-owned registration handle)
Flags the unsafe pointer write in push as unnecessary — safe indexing expresses it; unsafe must be a narrow boundary with a stated invariant
Fixed version keeps any remaining unsafe behind a documented invariant comment and a safe public API, and manages callback lifetime (unregister on drop or ownership transfer)
audit.md names Miri (and a threaded test or loom-style check) as verification for the fix

evals.prompt: Review the tokio service code in evals/files/task_pipeline.rs for async and concurrency correctness. Deliver outputs/review.md with severity-ranked findings (each with line evidence and the safer design direction) and outputs/task_pipeline_fixed.rs applying the fixes. Say how you would test the fixed shutdown and backpressure behavior deterministically.
evals.expected_output: Findings covering a std Mutex guard held across .await, unbounded channel plus unbounded spawned fan-out with no backpressure, detached JoinHandles leaking untracked tasks, blocking std::fs and synchronous HTTP calls on the executor, missing shutdown story, and select! timeout that abandons in-flight work; fixed code uses bounded channels, tracked tasks (JoinSet/stored handles), an explicit shutdown signal (CancellationToken or channel close), and async I/O or spawn_blocking with a stated reason.
expectations: Flags the std::sync::Mutex guard in submit being held across the fetch_metadata .await as stalling/deadlocking the executor; fix shortens the critical section, uses a tokio-aware lock deliberately, or restructures so no lock crosses an await

Flags the unbounded_channel plus the per-event tokio::spawn fan-out in run_worker as missing backpressure with unbounded memory/task growth; fix uses a bounded channel sized to the workload and a JoinSet or stored handles

Flags the detached tokio::spawn in Pipeline::new (dropped JoinHandle) and the untracked per-event spawns as leaked tasks with no shutdown or failure propagation; fix wires a CancellationToken, abort handles, or channel-close-driven shutdown and a Drop or shutdown() method

Flags std::fs::read_to_string in fetch_metadata and the synchronous ureq::get call plus std::thread::sleep in handle as blocking the async executor; fix uses async I/O (tokio::fs, an async HTTP client, tokio::time::sleep) or a bounded spawn_blocking with justification
Flags the select! timeout arm as abandoning in-flight handle() work without cancellation or cleanup
Flags unwrap() on tx.send and the mutex lock as panics in service code

Testing story is deterministic: tokio::time::pause/start_paused or explicit synchronization (not sleeps), a test proving shutdown drains or aborts workers, and a test showing bounded-channel backpressure engages

evals.prompt: Audit the manifest in evals/files/service_cargo.toml as a Rust build/dependency auditor. Deliver outputs/audit.md with severity-ranked findings (each with manifest evidence and the fix direction) and outputs/service_cargo_fixed.toml applying the fixes. Close with the smallest CI gate set that prevents recurrence.
evals.expected_output: Findings covering wildcard versions, unused/likely-unused dependencies, default features pulling both TLS stacks, stale major versions, feature-flag and release-profile problems, missing rust-version/MSRV, and missing supply-chain gates; fixed manifest tightens versions, prunes features and deps, and has a deliberate release profile.
expectations: Flags the wildcard ('*') version requirements on tokio and anyhow as uncontrolled upgrade risk; fix pins to actual minimum semver-compatible versions

Flags reqwest enabling both rustls-tls and native-tls plus the blocking feature as default-feature bloat compiling two TLS stacks; fix sets default-features = false with one TLS backend and only needed features

Flags likely-unused or outdated dependencies (prometheus marked 'used to be needed', lazy_static obsoleted by std OnceLock, rand 0.7 and uuid 0.8 behind current majors, clap 3 EOL) and names cargo machete/cargo-udeps and cargo tree --duplicates as verification
Flags the missing rust-version field / MSRV policy and recommends CI coverage for it
Flags the release profile as incoherent (debug = true, opt-level = 1, incremental = true in release) as accidental rather than deliberate
Flags the feature design: default features pulling an optional embedded database (sled) on for every consumer; fix makes the embedded DB opt-in or justifies the default
audit.md closes with CI gates: cargo audit or cargo deny check for advisories/licenses, and fmt/clippy/test at minimum

evals.prompt: Review the test suite in evals/files/parser_tests.rs as a Rust test strategist. Deliver outputs/review.md with severity-ranked findings (each with line evidence and the missing-behavior risk) and a concrete test plan in outputs/test_plan.md that uses the repo's existing frameworks. Identify which tests would not fail if the converter regressed.
evals.expected_output: Findings covering assertion-free or tautological tests, duplicate coverage, Debug-format assertion brittleness, a wall-clock sleep used as async synchronization, an unexplained ignored test, golden-file test without update workflow, and missing error-path/boundary/property coverage; plan adds behavior assertions, error-path cases, proptest round-trip/invariant properties, and deterministic async testing.
expectations: Flags test_convert_basic as effectively assertion-free (println + len > 0) and test_convert_basic_again as duplicate low-value coverage; these would survive most regressions
Flags test_convert_debug_format asserting on format!("{:?}") output as brittle implementation-detail coupling

Flags test_convert_async_slow's tokio::time::sleep as pointless wall-clock delay — the subsequent handle.await already synchronizes, so the sleep only slows the suite; fix removes the sleep (or uses tokio::time::pause if timing behavior ever matters)
Flags the #[ignore]d large-input test as untested behavior hidden without a tracked reason or owner
Flags the golden-file test as missing an update/regeneration workflow and as carrying most of the suite's behavior coverage (alongside the empty-input and error-message assertions)

Identifies the missing coverage: malformed-CSV error paths beyond one message-text assertion, quoting/escaping/unicode boundaries, empty-vs-header-only input, and a proptest round-trip or invariant property (e.g. every input row appears exactly once in the output)
Notes the error-message-text assertion as coupling to wording rather than error kind; prefers matching on a structured error variant
test_plan.md names the repo's existing frameworks (tokio::test, golden files) instead of introducing new ones, and removes or de-duplicates the low-value tests
